definitions.definition: The libertarian capacity of agents to choose otherwise under identical conditions, not wholly determined by prior causal events.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.8 — standard in philosophical discourse on libertarian free will, though alternative definitions exist

definitions.definition: The concept that agents themselves, not merely events, can originate new causal chains independently of deterministic physical processes.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.75 — coherent philosophical concept though debated in metaphysics

definitions.definition: The status of deserving praise or blame for actions based on having genuine alternative possibilities.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.8 — widely accepted in moral philosophy though contested by some compatibilists

definitions.definition: Non-physical dimensions or entities beyond the scope of empirical science.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.7 — conceptually clear but its ontological status is contested

assumptions.statement: A supernatural dimension exists, containing immaterial agents (souls) not wholly reducible to physical processes.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.7 — supported by classical theistic arguments (cosmological, teleological, moral) though empirical evidence is indirect

assumptions.statement: Human conscious experience exhibits agent causation where the self originates actions independently of deterministic physical processes.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.6 — based on introspective reports and philosophical interpretations, but contested by neuroscientific studies

assumptions.statement: Free will is defined as the libertarian capacity to choose otherwise under identical conditions.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.8 — standard in philosophical discourse on free will, though alternative definitions exist

assumptions.statement: Human societies exhibit robust practices of moral responsibility, attributing praise and blame to individuals.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.7 — widespread anthropological and sociological evidence, but interpretations vary

assumptions.statement: Moral responsibility entails alternative possibilities (the ability to do otherwise).
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.8 — standard in libertarian moral philosophy, though contested by compatibilists

evidence.summary: Introspective reports from decision-making tasks indicate a felt experience of genuine choice not determined by prior states.
evidence.source: Philosophical and psychological literature on volition
evidence.strength_justification: 0.65 — robust anecdotal data though subjective and potentially biased

evidence.summary: Near-death experiences report conscious agency operating independently of brain activity.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.55 — limited sample, controversial methodology, but non-zero evidence

evidence.summary: Theological traditions across major religions affirm that humans possess free will as part of divine design.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.75 — widespread agreement among theologians, though may reflect doctrinal bias

claims.statement: The presence of agent causation in human conscious experience provides evidence for the existence of libertarian free will.

inference_chain.text: Introspective reports indicate a felt experience of genuine choice not determined by prior states.
Human conscious experience exhibits agent causation that originates actions.
Near-death experiences report conscious agency operating independently of brain activity.
If conscious experience includes agent causation and free will is defined libertarianly, then libertarian free will exists.
The presence of agent causation in human conscious experience provides evidence for the existence of libertarian free will.

predictions.statement: If neuroscientific interventions show decisions are fully predetermined by unconscious neural processes before conscious awareness in a way that cannot be altered by agents, then the evidence for agent causation is undermined.
predictions.test: Use brain imaging and decision tasks to time-lock decision processes to unconscious neural activity.
predictions.decision_criterion: If more than 95% of decisions are initiated unconsciously consistently, the claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Readiness potentials precede conscious decisions in a majority of cases.

claims.statement: The existence of moral responsibility practices in human societies implies the existence of libertarian free will.

inference_chain.text: Human societies exhibit robust practices of moral responsibility.
Moral responsibility entails the ability to do otherwise.
If moral responsibility requires alternative possibilities and it exists, then alternative possibilities (free will) exist.
The existence of moral responsibility practices in human societies implies the existence of libertarian free will.

predictions.statement: If communities consistently hold individuals morally responsible while denying any genuine alternative possibilities, then this claim is falsified.
predictions.test: Conduct anthropological surveys of moral systems.
predictions.decision_criterion: If more than 50% of moral responsibility systems deny alternative possibilities while still practicing responsibility, the claim is falsified.

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A4 (0.7) and A5 (0.8); limited by A4 (0.7)

claims.statement: The convergence of theistic revelations affirming human free will indicates its existence.

inference_chain.text: A supernatural dimension containing immaterial agents exists.
Theological traditions across major religions affirm libertarian free will as part of divine design.
If a creator endowed humans with free will and authoritative traditions teach this, then free will exists.
The convergence of theistic revelations affirming human free will indicates its existence.

predictions.statement: If key religious texts are shown to not endorse the capacity to choose otherwise, then this claim is falsified.
predictions.test: Perform historical-critical exegesis of major scriptures.
predictions.decision_criterion: If no credible texts affirm libertarian free will, the claim is falsified.

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A1 (0.7), A3 (0.8), E3 (0.75); limited by A1 (0.7)

counterpositions.statement: Introspective and NDE reports of agent causation could be illusions produced by unconscious brain processes and lack objective verification.
counterpositions.my_response: While introspective reports require corroboration, systematic phenomenological studies and cross-method triangulation support genuine agent causation; further neuroscientific research can clarify.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilist theories demonstrate that moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes without requiring libertarian alternative possibilities.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist models reconceptualize responsibility but fail to account for the normative force of praise and blame that presupposes genuine alternative possibilities.

counterpositions.statement: Expert consensus among theologians is shaped by doctrinal traditions rather than independent evidence of free will.
counterpositions.my_response: Although traditions can reflect bias, the convergence of diverse independent traditions suggests a genuine metaphysical reality confirmed by revelation and reason.

uncertainties.question: Are introspective and NDE reports reliable indicators of true agent causation?
Is the link between moral responsibility and libertarian alternative possibilities necessary or can responsibility exist compatibly?
Do theological traditions provide independent epistemic warrant for free will or merely reflect doctrinal bias?

thesis.stance: Human beings possess libertarian free will. This is grounded in evidence of agent causation in conscious experience (C1), the indispensability of alternative possibilities for moral responsibility (C2), and the convergence of diverse theological revelations affirming free will (C3).
summary_bullets: Conscious experience exhibits agent causation not reducible to deterministic processes (C1).
Moral responsibility practices observed in societies necessitate the ability to choose otherwise (C2).
Diverse theistic traditions converge in affirming humans are endowed with free will (C3).
A supernatural dimension underwrites the metaphysical possibility of libertarian agency (A1).
